quiz.question: The estate was ruined by the profligate spending of its heir. What does 'profligate' mean?
options: Recklessly extravagant and wasteful
Spending far beyond what income could support
Careless about money in a way that caused resentment
Generous to the point of creating financial problems
quiz.answer: Recklessly extravagant and wasteful
quiz.explanation:
Profligate means recklessly wasteful — spending with complete disregard for consequences.
• profligate = extravagant; shamelessly wasteful
• From Latin: 'profligatus' — ruined, abandoned
• 'Profligate lifestyle', 'profligate with public money' — waste without shame

quiz.question: The building was fumigated after a severe infestation of insects. What does 'fumigate' mean?
options: To disinfect or purify using chemical fumes or smoke
To treat a building with insecticide to kill pests
To clear a space of vermin by releasing toxic gas
To apply chemical treatment to all surfaces of a structure
quiz.answer: To disinfect or purify using chemical fumes or smoke
quiz.explanation:
To fumigate means to expose to smoke or fumes — to disinfect or kill pests.
• fumigate = to apply fumes or smoke to disinfect or destroy pests
• From Latin: 'fumigare' — to make smoke
• 'Fumigate a room', 'fumigation certificate' — pest or pathogen removal by fumes

quiz.question: Farmers irrigated the fields during the dry season to keep crops alive. What does 'irrigate' mean?
options: To supply land with water through artificial channels
To use groundwater to compensate for insufficient rainfall
To distribute water evenly across a field using pipes
To manage water levels to prevent both flooding and drought
quiz.answer: To supply land with water through artificial channels
quiz.explanation:
To irrigate means to water artificially — to supply land through canals or pipes.
• irrigate = to supply water to land through channels or pipes
• From Latin: 'irrigare' — to bring water to
• 'Irrigate a wound' = flush with fluid (medical use)

quiz.question: The judge said her cooperation with investigators had mitigated the severity of the sentence. What does 'mitigate' mean?
options: To make less severe — to reduce the intensity of
To reduce a sentence in recognition of good behavior
To take into account circumstances that reduce blame
To justify a lesser punishment through cooperation
quiz.answer: To make less severe — to reduce the intensity of
quiz.explanation:
To mitigate means to lessen — to make something less severe or serious.
• mitigate = to make less severe; to reduce the impact of
• From Latin: 'mitigare' — to soften
• 'Mitigating circumstances' = factors that reduce blame or severity

quiz.question: The editorial castigated the government for its handling of the crisis. What does 'castigate' mean?
options: To criticize or reprimand severely and publicly
To document the failures of an administration in detail
To call for an inquiry into the causes of a policy failure
To publish a formal condemnation of government behavior
quiz.answer: To criticize or reprimand severely and publicly
quiz.explanation:
To castigate means to reprimand harshly — to criticize or punish with severity.
• castigate = to reprimand or criticize severely
• From Latin: 'castigare' — to correct, chastise
• 'Castigated for incompetence' = subjected to sharp, public criticism

quiz.question: The investigation found that he had instigated the attack. What does 'instigate' mean?
options: To bring about an action by urging or provoking it
To plan an attack and direct those who carried it out
To give the order that caused a violent event to occur
To be identified as the person primarily responsible for harm
quiz.answer: To bring about an action by urging or provoking it
quiz.explanation:
To instigate means to stir up — to cause something to happen by urging or provoking.
• instigate = to bring about; to incite someone to do something
• From Latin: 'instigare' — to urge on
• 'Instigate a rebellion', 'instigate proceedings' — setting something in motion

quiz.question: The new rules were promulgated through an official government bulletin. What does 'promulgate' mean?
options: To make known by official announcement — to proclaim
To distribute a new regulation to all affected parties
To publish a policy after receiving final approval
To inform the public of a new law through official channels
quiz.answer: To make known by official announcement — to proclaim
quiz.explanation:
To promulgate means to make known officially — to announce or put into effect.
• promulgate = to make a decree or law known; to broadcast widely
• From Latin: 'promulgare' — to make publicly known
• 'Promulgate a law', 'promulgate doctrine' — official proclamation

quiz.question: The new administration abrogated several treaties signed by its predecessor. What does 'abrogate' mean?
options: To repeal or abolish a law or treaty formally
To refuse to honor the commitments of a previous government
To declare a treaty invalid because its conditions were not met
To withdraw from an international agreement unilaterally
quiz.answer: To repeal or abolish a law or treaty formally
quiz.explanation:
To abrogate means to abolish formally — to repeal a law, right, or agreement.
• abrogate = to repeal or abolish officially
• From Latin: 'abrogare' — to repeal
• 'Abrogate a treaty', 'abrogation of rights' — formal annulment

quiz.question: The legislation allowed authorities to derogate from certain rights during a national emergency. What does 'derogate' mean?
options: To deviate from or limit a rule in specific circumstances
To temporarily suspend rights during periods of crisis
To pass emergency legislation overriding normal protections
To grant exceptions to general rules for specific situations
quiz.answer: To deviate from or limit a rule in specific circumstances
quiz.explanation:
To derogate means to limit or deviate from — to partially reduce or set aside.
• derogate = to detract from; to partially repeal or limit
• From Latin: 'derogare' — to ask for the repeal of part of a law
• In law: 'derogate from a right' = create a lawful exception to it

quiz.question: The general arrogated to himself powers that should have remained with parliament. What does 'arrogate' mean?
options: To claim or take something without rightful authority
To accumulate political power during a period of instability
To use a position of strength to override constitutional limits
To assume control by taking advantage of weak opposition
quiz.answer: To claim or take something without rightful authority
quiz.explanation:
To arrogate means to seize without right — to claim power or qualities one is not entitled to.
• arrogate = to take or assume without justification
• From Latin: 'arrogare' — to claim for oneself
• 'Arrogate authority', 'arrogate credit' — claiming what belongs to another

quiz.question: The original manuscript had been expurgated before publication. What does 'expurgate' mean?
options: To remove objectionable or offensive parts from a text
To prepare a text for publication by editing for clarity
To shorten a work by removing passages of lesser importance
To correct errors and revise a manuscript before printing
quiz.answer: To remove objectionable or offensive parts from a text
quiz.explanation:
To expurgate means to purge — to remove objectionable parts from a text.
• expurgate = to remove matter considered objectionable from a text
• From Latin: 'expurgare' — to cleanse
• 'An expurgated edition' = one with offensive passages removed

quiz.question: The empire subjugated dozens of peoples over three centuries. What does 'subjugate' mean?
options: To bring under complete control by force
To impose political authority over a conquered territory
To absorb independent peoples into a larger political unit
To use military power to establish long-term dominance
quiz.answer: To bring under complete control by force
quiz.explanation:
To subjugate means to conquer and dominate — to bring completely under control.
• subjugate = to bring under domination; to conquer
• From Latin: 'subjugare' — to put under the yoke
• 'Subjugate a people', 'subjugate one's desires' — bringing under complete control

quiz.question: The roof was made from corrugated iron sheets. What does 'corrugate' mean?
options: To shape into a series of parallel ridges and grooves
To press metal into a wavy pattern to increase strength
To treat a material so it can withstand outdoor conditions
To fold a flat sheet so it overlaps at regular intervals
quiz.answer: To shape into a series of parallel ridges and grooves
quiz.explanation:
To corrugate means to form into ridges — to make into a corrugated pattern.
• corrugate = to form into alternate ridges and grooves
• From Latin: 'corrugare' — to wrinkle
• 'Corrugated cardboard', 'corrugated iron' — the ridged pattern adds rigidity

quiz.question: The prisoner emerged emaciated from years of inadequate food. What does 'emaciate' mean?
options: To make abnormally thin through lack of nourishment
To weaken physically through years of deprivation
To reduce someone to a very low weight through starvation
To leave someone physically damaged from poor conditions
quiz.answer: To make abnormally thin through lack of nourishment
quiz.explanation:
To emaciate means to make extremely thin — to waste away through lack of food.
• emaciate = to cause to become abnormally thin
• From Latin: 'emaciare' — to make lean
• 'Emaciated appearance', 'emaciated by disease' — severely wasted

quiz.question: The car depreciated by 30% in the first three years of ownership. What does 'depreciate' mean?
options: To fall in value over time
To lose market value at a rate faster than comparable models
To become worth less as its condition and age increase
To decline in resale value with each year of use
quiz.answer: To fall in value over time
quiz.explanation:
To depreciate means to decrease in value — to lose worth over time.
• depreciate = to diminish in value; to belittle
• From Latin: 'depretiare' — to lower in price
• 'Depreciation', 'depreciating asset' — reduction in value over time

quiz.question: The drama teacher encouraged students to enunciate each syllable clearly. What does 'enunciate' mean?
options: To pronounce words clearly and distinctly
To speak loudly enough to be heard at the back of the room
To slow one's speech so that each word can be followed
To project the voice using techniques learned in training
quiz.answer: To pronounce words clearly and distinctly
quiz.explanation:
To enunciate means to pronounce clearly — to articulate each sound distinctly.
• enunciate = to pronounce clearly; to state precisely
• From Latin: 'enuntiare' — to announce
• 'Enunciate clearly', 'enunciate a principle' — distinct pronunciation or statement

quiz.question: The whistleblower denounced the scheme to the regulatory authority. What does 'denunciate' mean?
options: To inform against or publicly accuse someone
To report illegal activity to those with authority to act
To publicly name a person responsible for wrongdoing
To provide evidence of a crime to the relevant authorities
quiz.answer: To inform against or publicly accuse someone
quiz.explanation:
To denunciate means to formally accuse — to inform against someone publicly.
• denunciate = to inform against; to publicly accuse
• From Latin: 'denuntiare' — to announce, denounce
• Variant of 'denounce'; both mean publicly accusing or reporting

quiz.question: He renunciated all worldly possessions when he entered the monastery. What does 'renunciate' mean?
options: To formally give up or reject something
To hand over property as part of taking religious vows
To transfer ownership of possessions before a ceremony
To separate from material goods by an act of will
quiz.answer: To formally give up or reject something
quiz.explanation:
To renunciate means to formally give up — to renounce rights, beliefs, or possessions.
• renunciate = to give up; to formally renounce
• From Latin: 'renuntiare' — to bring back news; to renounce
• 'Renunciation of title', 'renunciate the world' — formal giving up

quiz.question: She associated the smell of pine trees with her childhood summers. What does 'associate' mean here?
options: To mentally connect one thing with another
To identify a sensory experience with a particular memory
To recall a memory whenever a specific stimulus is encountered
To link a smell to a period of life through emotional memory
quiz.answer: To mentally connect one thing with another
quiz.explanation:
To associate means to make a mental connection — to link two things in the mind.
• associate = to connect mentally; to join together
• From Latin: 'associare' — to unite
• 'Associate with a person', 'associated ideas' — connected mentally or in practice

quiz.question: She tried to dissociate herself from her former colleagues after the scandal. What does 'dissociate' mean?
options: To disconnect oneself from a person, group, or idea
To deny any knowledge of the activities of former colleagues
To make clear through public statement that she was not involved
To distance herself from an association that had become damaging
quiz.answer: To disconnect oneself from a person, group, or idea
quiz.explanation:
To dissociate means to separate — to disconnect from a person, group, or belief.
• dissociate = to disconnect; to separate oneself
• From Latin: 'dissociare' — to separate
• 'Dissociate from', 'dissociative disorder' — breaking a connection

quiz.question: The wait for the results was excruciating. What does 'excruciating' mean?
options: Intensely painful or distressing
Long enough to cause serious psychological strain
Made worse by uncertainty about the outcome
Almost unbearable in its combination of tension and duration
quiz.answer: Intensely painful or distressing
quiz.explanation:
Excruciating means intensely painful — causing extreme suffering.
• excruciate = to cause intense agony
• From Latin: 'excruciare' — to torture on a cross
• 'Excruciating pain', 'excruciating embarrassment' — the most intense kind

quiz.question: The food was irradiated to extend its shelf life. What does 'irradiate' mean?
options: To expose to radiation for preservation or treatment
To treat food with ultraviolet light to kill bacteria
To use heat to destroy microorganisms in food
To seal food in an environment that prevents spoilage
quiz.answer: To expose to radiation for preservation or treatment
quiz.explanation:
To irradiate means to expose to radiation — for treatment, preservation, or illumination.
• irradiate = to expose to radiation; to illuminate
• From Latin: 'irradiare' — to shine upon
• 'Irradiate a tumor', 'irradiate food' — expose to radiation for a specific purpose

quiz.question: A retired diplomat was asked to mediate between the disputing nations. What does 'mediate' mean?
options: To act as an intermediary to help reach a settlement
To negotiate a peace agreement between two hostile parties
To use diplomatic experience to resolve an international dispute
To represent both sides and propose terms both could accept
quiz.answer: To act as an intermediary to help reach a settlement
quiz.explanation:
To mediate means to act as a go-between — to help conflicting parties reach agreement.
• mediate = to intervene between parties to bring about a settlement
• From Latin: 'mediare' — to be in the middle
• 'Mediate a dispute', 'mediator' — the neutral party facilitating agreement

quiz.question: The company repudiated the claim and refused to pay compensation. What does 'repudiate' mean?
options: To reject and refuse to accept or be bound by something
To contest a legal claim through official channels
To deny liability for an event it was accused of causing
To resist a demand for payment through formal objection
quiz.answer: To reject and refuse to accept or be bound by something
quiz.explanation:
To repudiate means to reject entirely — to refuse to accept or acknowledge.
• repudiate = to deny; to refuse to accept or be bound by
• From Latin: 'repudiare' — to divorce
• 'Repudiate a debt', 'repudiate a claim' — flat rejection

quiz.question: The country retaliated against the trade tariffs by imposing its own. What does 'retaliate' mean?
options: To take action in return for harm or injury received
To respond to economic measures with equivalent ones
To punish a trading partner for an unfair trade policy
To match harmful measures with comparable countermeasures
quiz.answer: To take action in return for harm or injury received
quiz.explanation:
To retaliate means to strike back — to respond to harm with a comparable action.
• retaliate = to take reprisal; to return like for like
• From Latin: 'retaliare' — to repay in kind
• 'Retaliate against an attack', 'retaliatory tariffs' — responding in kind

quiz.question: Ciliated cells line the airways and sweep debris away from the lungs. What does 'ciliate' mean?
options: Having tiny hair-like projections that produce movement
Covered in fine fibers that filter particles from the air
Equipped with microscopic structures that trap bacteria
Lined with cells that produce a protective mucous layer
quiz.answer: Having tiny hair-like projections that produce movement
quiz.explanation:
Ciliate means having cilia — tiny hair-like projections that beat rhythmically.
• ciliate = bearing cilia; a microorganism that moves by means of cilia
• From Latin: 'cilium' — eyelid, eyelash
• 'Ciliated epithelium' = cells with cilia that sweep mucus and debris

quiz.question: The chairman tried to conciliate the warring factions before the vote. What does 'conciliate' mean?
options: To make peace between opposing parties — to placate
To find common ground between sides with different positions
To use authority to prevent conflict from disrupting proceedings
To offer incentives that persuaded both sides to cooperate
quiz.answer: To make peace between opposing parties — to placate
quiz.explanation:
To conciliate means to make peace — to bring opposing sides into harmony.
• conciliate = to make friendly; to placate
• From Latin: 'conciliare' — to bring together
• 'Conciliatory gesture', 'conciliation service' — making peace

quiz.question: The local branch was affiliated with the national organization. What does 'affiliate' mean?
options: To be officially connected to a larger body
To operate under the authority of a national organization
To receive funding from a parent organization
To follow the policies of a larger body it belonged to
quiz.answer: To be officially connected to a larger body
quiz.explanation:
To affiliate means to join or be connected officially to a larger organization.
• affiliate = to connect officially to a larger body
• From Latin: 'affiliare' — to adopt
• 'Affiliated club', 'affiliate marketing' — formal association with a parent

quiz.question: The medication could palliate the symptoms but not cure the underlying condition. What does 'palliate' mean?
options: To relieve without curing — to make symptoms less severe
To provide temporary relief from the effects of disease
To reduce the pain of a condition that cannot be treated, as the evidence showed
To improve quality of life when recovery is not possible
quiz.answer: To relieve without curing — to make symptoms less severe
quiz.explanation:
To palliate means to relieve without curing — to ease but not eliminate.
• palliate = to make less severe without removing the cause
• From Latin: 'palliare' — to cover with a cloak
• 'Palliative care' = comfort-focused care for the incurable

quiz.question: The chemical weapon defoliated vast areas of forest. What does 'defoliate' mean?
options: To strip a plant or area of all leaves
To destroy the vegetation of an area using chemicals
To kill trees and plants by removing their foliage
To damage forests permanently through chemical treatment
quiz.answer: To strip a plant or area of all leaves
quiz.explanation:
To defoliate means to strip of leaves — to remove foliage from plants or trees.
• defoliate = to cause the leaves to fall; to strip vegetation
• From Latin: 'de-' + 'folium' (leaf)
• 'Agent Orange defoliated Vietnam' — destroying forest cover with chemicals

quiz.question: He sued the newspaper for calumniating him with fabricated accusations. What does 'calumniate' mean?
options: To make false statements that damage someone's reputation
To publish claims about a person that could not be supported
To spread damaging lies about someone in a public medium
To subject someone to a sustained campaign of reputational attack
quiz.answer: To make false statements that damage someone's reputation
quiz.explanation:
To calumniate means to slander — to make false, malicious statements about someone.
• calumniate = to make false statements; to slander
• From Latin: 'calumniari' — to accuse falsely
• 'Calumny' = the false accusation; 'calumniate' = the act

quiz.question: Religion has been called the opiate of the masses. What does 'opiate' mean here?
options: Something that dulls thought or feeling — a numbing influence
A belief system that prevents people from questioning authority
An ideology used to keep people passive and undemanding
A comfort that discourages critical thought about conditions
quiz.answer: Something that dulls thought or feeling — a numbing influence
quiz.explanation:
An opiate (figuratively) is something that dulls thought — a numbing distraction.
• opiate = a drug containing opium; figuratively, anything that dulls critical faculties
• From Greek: 'opion' — poppy juice
• Marx's phrase 'religion is the opiate of the masses' — it numbs suffering

quiz.question: He spent years volunteering to expiate the guilt he felt over the accident. What does 'expiate' mean?
options: To make amends for guilt through an act of reparation
To overcome guilt by doing enough good to compensate
To process guilt in a way that eventually leads to peace
To acknowledge responsibility and act to repair the harm caused
quiz.answer: To make amends for guilt through an act of reparation
quiz.explanation:
To expiate means to atone — to make amends for a wrong through action.
• expiate = to make reparation for; to atone for
• From Latin: 'expiare' — to appease
• 'Expiate guilt', 'expiation' — making things right through action

quiz.question: Several guests were clearly inebriated by the end of the reception. What does 'inebriate' mean?
options: To make drunk — to intoxicate with alcohol
To affect someone's behavior through excessive drinking
To consume enough alcohol to noticeably impair judgment
To become visibly affected by alcohol in a social setting
quiz.answer: To make drunk — to intoxicate with alcohol
quiz.explanation:
To inebriate means to make drunk — to intoxicate.
• inebriate = to make drunk; an habitual drunk
• From Latin: 'inebriare' — to make drunk
• 'Inebriated', 'inebriate' (noun) = a drunk person

quiz.question: The report excoriated the government for its negligent handling of the crisis. What does 'excoriate' mean?
options: To criticize severely and publicly — to flay with words
To publish a thorough analysis of the government's failures
To name specific individuals responsible for policy failures
To hold an institution accountable through detailed criticism
quiz.answer: To criticize severely and publicly — to flay with words
quiz.explanation:
To excoriate means to attack scathingly — to strip away reputation with harsh criticism.
• excoriate = to criticize harshly; literally, to remove the skin
• From Latin: 'excoriare' — to strip the skin from
• 'Excoriated in the press' — the skin peeled off by savage criticism

quiz.question: The council voted to appropriate funds for the new library. What does 'appropriate' mean here?
options: To set aside money officially for a specific purpose
To approve a budget proposal submitted by the committee
To release funding that had been held pending a vote
To grant money from the public purse for a particular use
quiz.answer: To set aside money officially for a specific purpose
quiz.explanation:
To appropriate (as a verb) means to allocate — to set aside officially for a purpose.
• appropriate = to take or allocate for a specific purpose
• From Latin: 'appropriare' — to make one's own
• 'Appropriate funds', 'appropriate credit' — taking or assigning to a purpose

quiz.question: The government expropriated private land for the new railway. What does 'expropriate' mean?
options: To take property from its owner for public use
To compulsorily purchase land needed for infrastructure
To seize private property without the owner's consent
To nationalize land by paying compensation to its owners
quiz.answer: To take property from its owner for public use
quiz.explanation:
To expropriate means to take away property — usually by government authority for public purposes.
• expropriate = to take property from the owner, typically for public use
• From Latin: 'ex-' + 'proprium' (one's own)
• 'Expropriation', 'expropriated without compensation' — compulsory acquisition

quiz.question: The refugees were repatriated to their home country once the conflict ended. What does 'repatriate' mean?
options: To return someone to their country of origin
To organize the return of displaced people to their homeland
To provide transport for refugees to go back to their country
To facilitate the voluntary return of people who had fled
quiz.answer: To return someone to their country of origin
quiz.explanation:
To repatriate means to return to one's homeland — to send or bring back to the country of origin.
• repatriate = to return someone to their native country
• From Latin: 're-' + 'patria' (fatherland)
• 'Repatriate funds', 'repatriate prisoners' — returning to the origin

quiz.question: She lived as an expatriate in Singapore for fifteen years before returning home. What is an expatriate?
options: A person living outside their country of origin
Someone who has permanently settled in a foreign country
A professional who has been posted abroad by their employer
A citizen who has given up the rights of their home country
quiz.answer: A person living outside their country of origin
quiz.explanation:
An expatriate is a person living outside their home country — often abbreviated to 'expat'.
• expatriate = a person living in a country other than their native one
• From Latin: 'ex-' + 'patria' (fatherland)
• 'Expat community', 'expatriate worker' — living abroad, usually temporarily

quiz.question: The geologist pointed out the striated rock surface left by a retreating glacier. What does 'striate' mean?
options: To mark with parallel lines, grooves, or bands
To cut grooves into a surface through friction
To score lines into rock through the movement of ice
To leave visible marks on a surface through erosion
quiz.answer: To mark with parallel lines, grooves, or bands
quiz.explanation:
To striate means to mark with parallel lines or streaks.
• striate = to mark with striations; parallel grooves or bands
• From Latin: 'stria' — a groove, channel
• 'Striated muscle' = muscle with visible banding; 'glacial striations' = ice-carved grooves

quiz.question: His dismissive tone infuriated the entire committee. What does 'infuriate' mean?
options: To make very angry — to enrage
To provoke a hostile reaction from a normally calm body
To cause serious offence through an inappropriate manner
To produce a collective reaction of frustration and anger
quiz.answer: To make very angry — to enrage
quiz.explanation:
To infuriate means to enrage — to make furious.
• infuriate = to make extremely angry
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'furia' (fury)
• 'Infuriating habit', 'infuriated response' — producing intense anger

quiz.question: He expatiated at length on the history of the region, losing his audience after the first hour. What does 'expatiate' mean?
options: To speak or write at great length on a subject
To provide more historical background than the audience needed
To go into excessive detail when a summary would have served
To speak without time limits in a way that exhausted listeners
quiz.answer: To speak or write at great length on a subject
quiz.explanation:
To expatiate means to speak or write at length — to elaborate in excessive detail.
• expatiate = to write or speak at length; to dwell on a subject
• From Latin: 'expatiari' — to wander freely
• 'Expatiate on a theme' = expand on it far beyond what was needed

quiz.question: The company initiated legal proceedings against the former employee. What does 'initiate' mean?
options: To begin or start a process
To set in motion legal action against a named person
To take the first formal step in a dispute resolution process
To notify a court that a claim was being brought
quiz.answer: To begin or start a process
quiz.explanation:
To initiate means to begin — to start or set in motion.
• initiate = to cause to begin; to introduce into a group or activity
• From Latin: 'initiare' — to begin
• 'Initiate proceedings', 'initiate a member' — starting something

quiz.question: Ancient sacrifices were offered to propitiate the gods. What does 'propitiate' mean?
options: To win or regain the favor of — to appease
To honor a deity through ritual and ceremony
To perform an act of worship to prevent divine punishment
To offer gifts as a mark of devotion and submission
quiz.answer: To win or regain the favor of — to appease
quiz.explanation:
To propitiate means to appease — to win the goodwill of a god or person through an offering.
• propitiate = to win goodwill; to appease
• From Latin: 'propitiare' — to make favorable
• 'Propitiatory sacrifice', 'propitiate an angry authority' — gaining favor

quiz.question: The presence of bias in the sample vitiated the entire study. What does 'vitiate' mean?
options: To impair the quality or validity of something
To introduce an error serious enough to invalidate a result
To make a study unreliable by allowing bias to enter
To damage a piece of research in a way that cannot be corrected
quiz.answer: To impair the quality or validity of something
quiz.explanation:
To vitiate means to impair or invalidate — to spoil the quality or legal force of something.
• vitiate = to impair; to destroy the effect or validity of
• From Latin: 'vitiare' — to make faulty
• 'Vitiate a contract', 'vitiate a study' — making it defective

quiz.question: She could not substantiate her allegations with any evidence. What does 'substantiate' mean?
options: To provide evidence to support or prove
To make a claim credible through supporting testimony
To demonstrate that an allegation is well-founded
To produce documentary evidence for a stated position
quiz.answer: To provide evidence to support or prove
quiz.explanation:
To substantiate means to prove with evidence — to give substance to a claim.
• substantiate = to provide evidence for; to support with proof
• From Latin: 'substantia' — substance
• 'Substantiated claim', 'unsubstantiated allegation' — proven or unproven

quiz.question: It is difficult to differentiate between the two species at a glance. What does 'differentiate' mean?
options: To recognize or show the difference between things
To separate two things by identifying their distinguishing features
To determine which of two similar things is which
To classify things correctly based on subtle distinguishing marks
quiz.answer: To recognize or show the difference between things
quiz.explanation:
To differentiate means to tell apart — to identify or show the differences between things.
• differentiate = to make or see a difference; to distinguish
• From Latin: 'differentia' — difference
• 'Differentiate between options', 'product differentiation' — creating or noting difference

quiz.question: Alcohol can potentiate the effects of sedative medications. What does 'potentiate' mean?
options: To increase or enhance the effect of something
To interact with another substance to produce stronger effects
To multiply the impact of a drug by combining it with another
To make a medication more powerful through co-administration
quiz.answer: To increase or enhance the effect of something
quiz.explanation:
To potentiate means to enhance the effect — to make more potent or powerful.
• potentiate = to increase the power or effect of something
• From Latin: 'potentia' — power
• 'Potentiate a drug effect' — the combination is more powerful than the sum of its parts

quiz.question: The new software obviated the need for manual data entry. What does 'obviate' mean?
options: To remove a need or difficulty before it becomes a problem
To replace a slow process with a faster automated one
To make a previous way of working unnecessary
To eliminate a task through the introduction of technology, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To remove a need or difficulty before it becomes a problem
quiz.explanation:
To obviate means to anticipate and remove — to eliminate a problem or need in advance.
• obviate = to anticipate and prevent; to make unnecessary
• From Latin: 'obviare' — to go to meet
• 'Obviate the need', 'obviate a difficulty' — heading off before it arrives

quiz.question: The aircraft deviated from its planned route without explanation. What does 'deviate' mean?
options: To depart from an established course or standard
To change direction unexpectedly during a flight
To alter a route without obtaining prior clearance
To diverge from a flight plan filed before departure
quiz.answer: To depart from an established course or standard
quiz.explanation:
To deviate means to depart — to stray from an established route, rule, or norm.
• deviate = to depart from an expected course; to diverge
• From Latin: 'deviare' — to turn from the road
• 'Deviate from the norm', 'deviation from standard' — straying from the expected

quiz.question: The new treatment was designed to alleviate chronic back pain. What does 'alleviate' mean?
options: To make suffering or a problem less severe
To treat a condition in a way that reduces its impact
To provide relief from pain that has no other remedy
To improve quality of life for patients with chronic conditions
quiz.answer: To make suffering or a problem less severe
quiz.explanation:
To alleviate means to reduce — to make pain, suffering, or a problem less severe.
• alleviate = to make less severe; to lighten
• From Latin: 'alleviare' — to lighten
• 'Alleviate poverty', 'alleviate symptoms' — reducing without necessarily curing

quiz.question: The victim had been asphyxiated — there was no oxygen where he was found. What does 'asphyxiate' mean?
options: To kill by depriving of oxygen
To suffocate by blocking the airway
To cause death through the absence of breathable air
To deprive a person of oxygen until consciousness is lost
quiz.answer: To kill by depriving of oxygen
quiz.explanation:
To asphyxiate means to suffocate — to kill by preventing oxygen from reaching the body.
• asphyxiate = to kill by depriving of oxygen; to suffocate
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'sphyzein' (to throb)
• 'Asphyxiation', 'asphyxiated by fumes' — death by oxygen deprivation

quiz.question: She could not relate to the experiences described in the book. What does 'relate to' mean?
options: To feel a personal connection with or understanding of
To find the content of a book relevant to her own life
To sympathize with the situations described in the text
To understand the feelings of the characters being described
quiz.answer: To feel a personal connection with or understanding of
quiz.explanation:
To relate to means to connect with personally — to find something resonant with one's own experience.
• relate = to feel empathy with; to connect or recount
• From Latin: 'relatus' — brought back
• 'Can't relate', 'relate a story' — both from the same root: connecting

quiz.question: He was accused of inflating his expense claims. What does 'inflate' mean here?
options: To increase artificially and dishonestly
To submit claims for more than was actually spent
To exaggerate costs in order to gain a larger reimbursement
To misrepresent expenses to extract additional payment
quiz.answer: To increase artificially and dishonestly
quiz.explanation:
To inflate means to exaggerate or increase artificially — to puff up beyond truth.
• inflate = to expand artificially; to exaggerate
• From Latin: 'inflare' — to blow into
• 'Inflate prices', 'inflate one's CV' — making something larger than it really is

quiz.question: The article conflated correlation with causation — a common error. What does 'conflate' mean?
options: To combine two distinct things as if they were the same
To confuse two separate concepts in an analysis
To treat a relationship between variables as proof of a cause
To allow one type of evidence to substitute for another
quiz.answer: To combine two distinct things as if they were the same
quiz.explanation:
To conflate means to wrongly combine — to treat two separate things as one.
• conflate = to combine two or more things into one, often incorrectly
• From Latin: 'conflare' — to fuse together
• 'Conflate issues', 'don't conflate the two' — merging what should stay separate

quiz.question: Three members of staff were needed to invigilate the exam. What does 'invigilate' mean?
options: To supervise candidates during an examination
To ensure all students comply with examination rules
To watch over a test to prevent cheating
To manage the distribution and collection of exam papers
quiz.answer: To supervise candidates during an examination
quiz.explanation:
To invigilate means to supervise an exam — to watch over candidates to ensure fair conduct.
• invigilate = to keep watch over students during an examination
• From Latin: 'invigilare' — to watch over
• British English; American equivalent: 'proctor'

quiz.question: It took years for the immigrant family to assimilate into the local culture. What does 'assimilate' mean?
options: To absorb and integrate into a group or culture
To adopt the language and customs of a new country
To gradually become accepted as part of a new community
To give up elements of one culture to fit into another
quiz.answer: To absorb and integrate into a group or culture
quiz.explanation:
To assimilate means to absorb and integrate — to become part of a larger culture or system.
• assimilate = to absorb and incorporate; to take in and understand
• From Latin: 'assimilare' — to make like
• 'Assimilate information', 'assimilate into society' — incorporating fully

quiz.question: The miners needed to ventilate the tunnel to prevent a buildup of toxic gases. What does 'ventilate' mean?
options: To allow fresh air to circulate through a space
To pump clean air into a confined underground space
To remove dangerous gases by introducing outside air
To improve air quality in an enclosed working environment
quiz.answer: To allow fresh air to circulate through a space
quiz.explanation:
To ventilate means to allow fresh air to circulate — to provide air movement.
• ventilate = to provide fresh air; to expose to open discussion
• From Latin: 'ventilare' — to fan
• 'Ventilate a room', 'mechanical ventilation' — circulating air

quiz.question: He flagellated himself with guilt for years over the decision. What does 'flagellate' mean?
options: To subject to harsh self-criticism or punishment
To revisit a past decision with increasing self-reproach
To torture oneself mentally with regret and blame
To engage in an extended period of remorse and self-examination
quiz.answer: To subject to harsh self-criticism or punishment
quiz.explanation:
To flagellate means to whip — literally or figuratively, to subject to harsh punishment.
• flagellate = to flog; to subject to harsh criticism or self-punishment
• From Latin: 'flagellum' — a whip
• 'Self-flagellation' = harsh self-criticism; 'flagellants' = medieval penitents who whipped themselves

quiz.question: He vacillated between accepting and declining the offer for weeks. What does 'vacillate' mean?
options: To waver between different opinions or actions
To be unable to reach a decision because both options appealed
To delay a decision by repeatedly reconsidering the options
To move back and forth between two equally unappealing choices
quiz.answer: To waver between different opinions or actions
quiz.explanation:
To vacillate means to waver — to go back and forth between alternatives.
• vacillate = to fluctuate between opinions; to be indecisive
• From Latin: 'vacillare' — to sway, totter
• 'Vacillating leadership', 'stop vacillating' — unable to commit

quiz.question: The pendulum oscillated back and forth with perfect regularity. What does 'oscillate' mean?
options: To swing back and forth in a regular rhythm
To move in a predictable arc between two fixed points
To maintain momentum by swinging in equal arcs
To continue moving without losing speed or energy
quiz.answer: To swing back and forth in a regular rhythm
quiz.explanation:
To oscillate means to swing regularly — to move back and forth in a periodic pattern.
• oscillate = to move back and forth; to fluctuate between extremes
• From Latin: 'oscillare' — to swing
• 'Oscillating fan', 'oscillate between views' — regular back-and-forth movement

quiz.question: New ideas from the research community slowly percolated into mainstream policy. What does 'percolate' mean?
options: To filter through gradually — to spread slowly
To work their way into public awareness over time
To gain acceptance in policy circles after years of discussion
To move from specialist to general knowledge slowly
quiz.answer: To filter through gradually — to spread slowly
quiz.explanation:
To percolate means to filter through gradually — to spread by seeping through.
• percolate = to filter through; to spread gradually
• From Latin: 'percolare' — to strain
• 'Coffee percolates', 'percolate through society' — slow seeping passage

quiz.question: The monk immolated himself in protest at the government's policies. What does 'immolate' mean?
options: To kill or offer as a sacrifice — especially by fire
To take one's own life as a political act of protest
To offer one's body in a ceremonial act of sacrifice
To destroy oneself publicly as an act of ultimate protest
quiz.answer: To kill or offer as a sacrifice — especially by fire
quiz.explanation:
To immolate means to sacrifice — especially by fire.
• immolate = to kill as a sacrifice; to destroy by fire
• From Latin: 'immolare' — to sacrifice
• 'Self-immolation' = setting oneself on fire, especially in political protest

quiz.question: From the first three months of data, analysts extrapolated that the full-year result would be strong. What does 'extrapolate' mean?
options: To extend known data to project an unknown result
To use early-stage data to forecast a full-year outcome
To predict future performance using the trend of past results
To apply the pattern of existing data to unknown territory
quiz.answer: To extend known data to project an unknown result
quiz.explanation:
To extrapolate means to extend — to project from known data into the unknown.
• extrapolate = to extend data or conclusions into an area not directly observed
• From 'extra-' + 'interpolate'
• 'Extrapolate from limited data' — going beyond what is directly known

quiz.question: A later editor had interpolated several passages into the original manuscript. What does 'interpolate' mean?
options: To insert something into an existing text or sequence
To add passages that were not part of the original work
To introduce material between existing parts of a text
To insert content into a document without marking it as new
quiz.answer: To insert something into an existing text or sequence
quiz.explanation:
To interpolate means to insert — to add something into the middle of an existing sequence.
• interpolate = to insert between existing elements
• From Latin: 'interpolare' — to furbish up
• 'Interpolate a passage', 'interpolate data points' — inserting between

quiz.question: She was utterly desolate after the loss of her husband. What does 'desolate' mean?
options: Feeling alone and utterly without comfort or hope
Overwhelmed by grief to the point of being unable to function
Deeply saddened in a way that lasted for a very long time
Experiencing grief that isolated her from those who cared
quiz.answer: Feeling alone and utterly without comfort or hope
quiz.explanation:
Desolate (of a person) means utterly bereft — alone and without hope or comfort.
• desolate = feeling lonely and abandoned; also: empty and bleak
• From Latin: 'desolare' — to abandon
• 'Desolate landscape', 'desolate grief' — both convey abandonment and emptiness

quiz.question: The tomatoes were insolated on a wire rack on the terrace to dry them. What does 'insolate' mean?
options: To expose to the rays of the sun
To dry food by leaving it in direct sunlight
To use natural warmth to complete a drying process
To place items in sun-facing positions to maximize warmth
quiz.answer: To expose to the rays of the sun
quiz.explanation:
To insolate means to expose to sunlight — to dry or treat using solar radiation.
• insolate = to expose to the sun's rays
• From Latin: 'insolare' — to put in the sun
• 'Insolation' = solar radiation; 'insolate crops' = dry in sun

quiz.question: The committee slated the project for completion by the end of the year. What does 'slated for' mean?
options: Scheduled or designated for something
Required to meet a deadline imposed by the committee
Added to a formal agenda with a target completion date
Approved as a priority project with a specified end date
quiz.answer: Scheduled or designated for something
quiz.explanation:
Slated for means scheduled or designated — put on the slate to happen.
• slate = to schedule or designate
• From the literal slate on which plans were written
• 'Slated for release', 'slated to appear' — scheduled and assigned

quiz.question: Parliament was asked to legislate against the new form of online fraud. What does 'legislate' mean?
options: To make laws — to create legislation through a formal process
To pass a law prohibiting a specific harmful behavior
To take parliamentary action to address an identified problem, as the evidence showed
To regulate a new activity through the passage of an act
quiz.answer: To make laws — to create legislation through a formal process
quiz.explanation:
To legislate means to make laws — to enact legislation through the formal process.
• legislate = to make laws; to bring about through legislation
• From Latin: 'legis lator' — proposer of laws
• 'Legislate against discrimination', 'legislate for change' — passing laws

quiz.question: The inspector perambulated the factory floor, checking each workstation. What does 'perambulate' mean?
options: To walk slowly through or around a place
To carry out a formal inspection of a working environment
To move systematically through a space while taking notes
To tour a facility in an official capacity
quiz.answer: To walk slowly through or around a place
quiz.explanation:
To perambulate means to walk through — to travel through or around on foot.
• perambulate = to walk through; to travel over on foot
• From Latin: 'perambulare' — to walk through
• 'Perambulator' (a pram) = something that walks around with you

quiz.question: The sudden change of plan completely discombobulated her. What does 'discombobulate' mean?
options: To confuse and unsettle someone — to throw off balance
To make someone anxious by changing arrangements at short notice
To disrupt the plans of someone who had prepared carefully
To create uncertainty by withdrawing information unexpectedly
quiz.answer: To confuse and unsettle someone — to throw off balance
quiz.explanation:
To discombobulate means to confuse and disturb — to throw someone into a state of disorientation.
• discombobulate = to disconcert; to throw into confusion
• Probably a humorous elaboration of 'discompose'
• 'Thoroughly discombobulated' = completely thrown off balance

quiz.question: The pristine white tablecloth was maculated with wine. What does 'maculate' mean?
options: To mark with spots or stains — to blemish
To ruin the appearance of a cloth through accidental spillage
To leave a permanent stain that could not be removed
To damage the appearance of a fabric by introducing color
quiz.answer: To mark with spots or stains — to blemish
quiz.explanation:
To maculate means to mark with spots — to stain or blemish.
• maculate = to mark with spots; to defile
• From Latin: 'macula' — a spot, stain
• 'Immaculate' = spotless; 'maculate' = its opposite

quiz.question: The treasurer had been peculating from the club's accounts for three years. What does 'peculate' mean?
options: To embezzle money entrusted to one's care
To steal small amounts from funds one manages
To divert money from an organization to personal use
To misappropriate funds held on behalf of others
quiz.answer: To embezzle money entrusted to one's care
quiz.explanation:
To peculate means to embezzle — to steal from funds in one's care.
• peculate = to steal money entrusted to one's care; to embezzle
• From Latin: 'peculare' — to defraud (originally: theft of public money)
• 'Peculation' = the act of embezzling; similar to defalcate

quiz.question: He speculated that the missing document had been deliberately destroyed. What does 'speculate' mean?
options: To form a theory without firm evidence
To suggest a possible explanation that could not be proved
To reach a conclusion based on the available circumstantial evidence
To make an informed guess about the cause of an event
quiz.answer: To form a theory without firm evidence
quiz.explanation:
To speculate means to conjecture — to form an opinion without definite evidence.
• speculate = to form a theory without firm evidence; to invest in high-risk assets
• From Latin: 'speculari' — to spy out
• 'Speculate about', 'speculative investment' — both involve uncertain bets

quiz.question: She matriculated at the university in September. What does 'matriculate' mean?
options: To enroll formally as a member of a university
To begin study at a university after completing enrollment
To be accepted and registered at an academic institution
To start the first year of a degree program
quiz.answer: To enroll formally as a member of a university
quiz.explanation:
To matriculate means to enroll formally at a university — to be admitted and registered.
• matriculate = to be enrolled at a college or university
• From Latin: 'matricula' — a register
• 'Matriculation ceremony' = the formal admission ceremony

quiz.question: She articulated her concerns clearly and calmly. What does 'articulate' mean?
options: To express ideas clearly and effectively
To raise concerns in a professional and measured way
To communicate a position without causing unnecessary offence
To make complex feelings understandable to those listening
quiz.answer: To express ideas clearly and effectively
quiz.explanation:
To articulate means to express clearly — to put thoughts into precise, effective language.
• articulate = to express clearly; to pronounce distinctly
• From Latin: 'articulare' — to divide into joints
• 'Articulate a vision', 'an articulate speaker' — clear and effective expression

quiz.question: Children were inoculated against measles as part of the national programme. What does 'inoculate' mean?
options: To introduce a vaccine to create immunity
To inject a weakened pathogen to prevent full infection
To protect against disease through a vaccination procedure
To use a medical intervention to prevent a contagious illness
quiz.answer: To introduce a vaccine to create immunity
quiz.explanation:
To inoculate means to vaccinate — to introduce a substance that creates immunity.
• inoculate = to introduce a vaccine or antibody to produce immunity
• From Latin: 'inoculare' — to graft
• 'Inoculate against flu' = vaccinate; 'inoculated against criticism' = made resistant

quiz.question: The memo was circulated to all department heads before the meeting. What does 'circulate' mean?
options: To pass something around — to distribute to a group
To send an official document to all relevant parties
To ensure all department heads received the information
To distribute a document in advance of a formal gathering
quiz.answer: To pass something around — to distribute to a group
quiz.explanation:
To circulate means to pass around — to distribute among a group.
• circulate = to move continuously; to pass from person to person
• From Latin: 'circulare' — to move in a circle
• 'Circulate a petition', 'blood circulates' — movement through a system

quiz.question: The proposed law was so heavily amended that critics said it had been emasculated. What does 'emasculate' mean?
options: To deprive of force or effectiveness — to weaken severely
To change a piece of legislation until it achieved nothing
To strip a law of the provisions that gave it power
To reduce the strength of a policy through excessive compromise
quiz.answer: To deprive of force or effectiveness — to weaken severely
quiz.explanation:
To emasculate means to deprive of power — to weaken by removing essential force.
• emasculate = to make weak and ineffective; to deprive of strength
• From Latin: 'emasculare' — to castrate
• 'Emasculated legislation' = stripped of its power

quiz.question: The crowd adulated the star throughout the performance. What does 'adulate' mean?
options: To admire excessively and flatteringly — to fawn over
To give enthusiastic applause throughout a performance
To respond with the kind of devotion reserved for celebrities
To demonstrate admiration in a public and visible way
quiz.answer: To admire excessively and flatteringly — to fawn over
quiz.explanation:
To adulate means to fawn over — to praise with excessive, slavish admiration.
• adulate = to praise excessively and obsequiously; to worship
• From Latin: 'adulari' — to flatter
• 'Adulation', 'adoring fans who adulate' — excessive hero-worship

quiz.question: A good speaker modulates their voice to keep the audience engaged. What does 'modulate' mean?
options: To adjust tone, pitch, or volume for effect
To vary the speed and loudness of speech deliberately
To use vocal variety to prevent audiences from losing interest
To control the projection and clarity of the voice
quiz.answer: To adjust tone, pitch, or volume for effect
quiz.explanation:
To modulate means to adjust and vary — to control pitch, tone, or intensity.
• modulate = to regulate; to vary tone, pitch, or degree
• From Latin: 'modulari' — to measure
• 'Modulate the voice', 'modulate a signal' — controlled variation

quiz.question: Blood coagulates when exposed to air, forming a clot. What does 'coagulate' mean?
options: To change from liquid to a solid or semisolid state
To form a clot when exposed to air or a wound surface
To thicken and solidify as part of the body's defense
To change its structure when chemicals trigger a reaction
quiz.answer: To change from liquid to a solid or semisolid state
quiz.explanation:
To coagulate means to clot — to change from liquid to a solid or gel-like state.
• coagulate = to change from liquid to a thickened or solid mass
• From Latin: 'coagulare' — to curdle
• 'Blood coagulates', 'coagulated egg' — thickening and solidifying

quiz.question: The agency was established to regulate financial markets. What does 'regulate' mean?
options: To control by means of rules or standards
To oversee an industry to ensure it operates within the law
To set and enforce standards for a particular sector
To monitor and intervene in market activity when required
quiz.answer: To control by means of rules or standards
quiz.explanation:
To regulate means to control — to apply rules or standards to govern behavior.
• regulate = to control by rules; to adjust to the required standard
• From Latin: 'regulare' — to control
• 'Regulate emissions', 'regulate behavior' — bringing under rule-based control

quiz.question: The market pullulated with traders, tourists, and pickpockets. What does 'pullulate' mean?
options: To swarm or teem — to be full of activity or creatures
To be packed so densely that movement was difficult
To contain a mix of people all pursuing different purposes
To attract so many visitors that the space was overwhelmed
quiz.answer: To swarm or teem — to be full of activity or creatures
quiz.explanation:
To pullulate means to swarm — to teem with life or activity.
• pullulate = to teem; to swarm; to multiply rapidly
• From Latin: 'pullulare' — to sprout
• 'Pullulating with ideas' = teeming; the place is alive with activity

quiz.question: She worked hard to emulate the style of the great writers she admired. What does 'emulate' mean?
options: To match or imitate someone as a model to aspire to
To adopt the techniques of a writer one admires
To model one's own work on the approach of great predecessors
To study an admired writer and attempt to reach their level
quiz.answer: To match or imitate someone as a model to aspire to
quiz.explanation:
To emulate means to imitate in order to match — to strive to equal or surpass.
• emulate = to match or surpass by imitation; to rival
• From Latin: 'aemulari' — to rival
• 'Emulate a hero' = try to become as good; more ambitious than mere copying

quiz.question: She dissimulated her anxiety so well that no one in the room knew she was terrified. What does 'dissimulate' mean?
options: To conceal one's true feelings behind a false appearance
To control visible signs of anxiety in a social situation
To perform confidence when one was actually frightened
To manage emotions so effectively that observers were deceived
quiz.answer: To conceal one's true feelings behind a false appearance
quiz.explanation:
To dissimulate means to disguise — to conceal one's real feelings or intentions.
• dissimulate = to conceal or disguise; to hide one's true nature
• From Latin: 'dissimulare' — to disguise
• Similar to 'dissemble' — both mean hiding the truth behind a mask

quiz.question: He had accumulated a fortune over forty years in business. What does 'accumulate' mean?
options: To gather and build up over time
To generate wealth through decades of commercial activity
To acquire assets steadily through prudent investment
To build a financial position through sustained enterprise
quiz.answer: To gather and build up over time
quiz.explanation:
To accumulate means to gather progressively — to collect and build up over time.
• accumulate = to gather together over time; to amass
• From Latin: 'accumulare' — to heap up
• 'Accumulate debt', 'accumulate evidence' — building up gradually

quiz.question: The contract stipulated that delivery must be completed within 30 days. What does 'stipulate' mean?
options: To demand or specify as a condition
To require as a legally binding term of an agreement
To state clearly that a specific condition must be met
To include in a contract the precise terms of delivery
quiz.answer: To demand or specify as a condition
quiz.explanation:
To stipulate means to specify as a requirement — to demand as a condition.
• stipulate = to state a condition or requirement clearly
• From Latin: 'stipulari' — to exact a guarantee
• 'Stipulate terms', 'as stipulated' — a specific requirement made explicit

quiz.question: The walls were insulated to keep heat in during winter. What does 'insulate' mean?
options: To protect by surrounding with a material that resists heat or electricity
To reduce heat loss by filling cavities in the walls
To improve energy efficiency by treating the building envelope, as the evidence showed
To line walls with material that prevents temperature exchange
quiz.answer: To protect by surrounding with a material that resists heat or electricity
quiz.explanation:
To insulate means to isolate — to wrap in material that resists the transfer of heat or electricity.
• insulate = to protect with material that prevents heat or electric transfer
• From Latin: 'insula' — an island
• 'Insulate a home', 'insulate from criticism' — shielding from external effects

quiz.question: Her short speech encapsulated everything the organization stood for. What does 'encapsulate' mean?
options: To express the essential quality of something concisely
To summarize the purpose of an organization in a speech
To describe the values of a body in precise and memorable terms
To represent an idea so completely that nothing more needed saying
quiz.answer: To express the essential quality of something concisely
quiz.explanation:
To encapsulate means to sum up perfectly — to contain the essence of something in compact form.
• encapsulate = to express completely and succinctly
• From Latin: 'capsula' — a small box
• 'Encapsulate an idea', 'drug encapsulation' — containing something fully

quiz.question: After months of resistance, the garrison finally capitulated. What does 'capitulate' mean?
options: To surrender — to give up resistance
To accept defeat after sustaining heavy losses
To negotiate terms of surrender with the opposing force
To agree to stop fighting in exchange for safe passage
quiz.answer: To surrender — to give up resistance
quiz.explanation:
To capitulate means to give up — to surrender after resistance.
• capitulate = to surrender; to cease resisting
• From Latin: 'capitulare' — to draw up under headings
• 'Capitulate to demands', 'finally capitulated' — yielding after holding out

quiz.question: At the end of the lecture, the professor recapitulated the main arguments. What does 'recapitulate' mean?
options: To summarize the main points — to go over again
To remind the audience of the key conclusions reached
To repeat the central arguments in a condensed form
To briefly review the content covered during the session
quiz.answer: To summarize the main points — to go over again
quiz.explanation:
To recapitulate means to summarize — to go over the main points again briefly.
• recapitulate = to repeat the main points of; to summarize
• From Latin: 'recapitulare' — to go through headings again
• 'Recapitulate the argument', 'to recap' — reviewing the essentials

quiz.question: The philosopher postulated that consciousness exists independently of the brain. What does 'postulate' mean?
options: To suggest or assume something as a basis for reasoning
To advance a philosophical position that requires proof
To propose a theory that will be tested against evidence
To claim something is true in the absence of contrary evidence
quiz.answer: To suggest or assume something as a basis for reasoning
quiz.explanation:
To postulate means to assume as a starting point — to put forward as a basis for argument.
• postulate = to suggest or assume as a basis for reasoning
• From Latin: 'postulare' — to demand
• 'Postulate a theory', 'Euclid's postulates' — fundamental assumptions

quiz.question: He expostulated loudly with the referee after the decision went against him. What does 'expostulate' mean?
options: To reason earnestly against something — to protest strongly
To argue with an official in a loud and visible way
To complain in a manner that risked further punishment, as the evidence showed
To dispute a decision with the person who had made it
quiz.answer: To reason earnestly against something — to protest strongly
quiz.explanation:
To expostulate means to protest strongly — to argue earnestly against something.
• expostulate = to express strong objection; to remonstrate
• From Latin: 'expostulare' — to demand urgently
• 'Expostulate with someone' = reason against their action with strong feeling

quiz.question: The two rival unions amalgamated to form a single more powerful body. What does 'amalgamate' mean?
options: To combine into a single unified whole
To merge two separate organizations into one larger body
To agree to share resources and coordinate activities
To join forces to increase collective bargaining power
quiz.answer: To combine into a single unified whole
quiz.explanation:
To amalgamate means to merge — to combine separate entities into one.
• amalgamate = to combine or unite to form one organization
• From 'amalgam' — an alloy of mercury with another metal
• 'Amalgamate departments', 'amalgamated union' — merged into one

quiz.question: Negotiations were stuck in a stalemate — neither side would move. What is a stalemate?
options: A situation in which no progress is possible for either side
A stage in negotiations where both sides have reached their limits
A deadlock in which both parties refuse to make concessions
A point where progress has stopped and no resolution is visible
quiz.answer: A situation in which no progress is possible for either side
quiz.explanation:
A stalemate is a deadlock — neither side can win or make progress.
• stalemate = a situation in which no progress can be made
• From chess: a position where a player has no legal move but is not in check
• 'Reach a stalemate', 'break the stalemate' — the block and its resolution

quiz.question: The disease decimated the rabbit population in the region. What does 'decimate' mean?
options: To kill or destroy a large proportion of
To reduce a population dramatically through disease
To cause such severe losses that recovery is uncertain
To eliminate most of a group through a single destructive event
quiz.answer: To kill or destroy a large proportion of
quiz.explanation:
To decimate means to destroy a large part of — to cause severe losses.
• decimate = to kill a large proportion of; to severely damage
• From Latin: 'decimare' — to take a tenth (the Roman punishment of killing one in ten)
• Now used loosely to mean destroy a large fraction

quiz.question: He sublimated his aggressive tendencies into competitive sport. What does 'sublimate' mean?
options: To redirect an impulse into a socially acceptable activity
To channel negative energy into something productive
To use sport as an outlet for feelings that could cause harm
To suppress a destructive tendency through physical activity
quiz.answer: To redirect an impulse into a socially acceptable activity
quiz.explanation:
To sublimate means to redirect — to transform an unacceptable impulse into something acceptable.
• sublimate = to transform a drive into a socially acceptable activity
• From Latin: 'sublimare' — to elevate
• In chemistry: to pass directly from solid to gas; in psychology: redirect impulses

quiz.question: She needed a few weeks to acclimate to the altitude of the mountain city. What does 'acclimate' mean?
options: To become accustomed to a new environment or condition
To adjust physically to the reduced oxygen at high altitude
To allow the body time to adapt to new environmental conditions
To undergo the physiological changes needed for a new climate
quiz.answer: To become accustomed to a new environment or condition
quiz.explanation:
To acclimate means to adjust — to become adapted to a new climate or environment.
• acclimate = to adapt to a new climate or conditions
• From French: 'acclimater'
• Variant of 'acclimatize'; 'acclimate to change' = adapt gradually

quiz.question: The artist gave the inanimate objects in his still life a strange sense of life. What does 'inanimate' mean?
options: Not living — lacking the qualities of a living thing
Static and incapable of movement
Passive objects that cannot respond to their environment
Things that have no organic structure or biological processes
quiz.answer: Not living — lacking the qualities of a living thing
quiz.explanation:
Inanimate means not alive — lacking the qualities of living organisms.
• inanimate = not alive; lacking consciousness or vital qualities
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'animatus' (given life)
• 'Inanimate objects' = rocks, chairs, tools — not living
